dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to unknown problem/person down at redacted address. Patient is reported unresponsive in a vehicle. Equip with BVM, naloxone, and EKG.
Hello, I'm Mark, an EMT. My partner is Liz. What led to the 911 call?
My partner started driving, fell asleep then seemed to stop breathing. He became unresponsive, so I called 911.
Can he follow your voice or open his eyes without stimulation?
He doesn't respond unless we physically touch or press on his shoulder.
Pt opens eyes to pain, but does not follow commands or verbal cues.
Pupils are 2 mm and equal.
Airway is patent. No signs of obstruction or abnormal sound.
Pt has shallow, snoring respirations. Bilateral chest rise and fall observed.
Respiratory rate is 8 per minute.
SpO2 is 75% while on room air.
Initiating assisted ventilation using BVM with 15 liters per minute of oxygen.
Ventilation via BVM in progress to improve oxygenation.
Pt has a pulse of 90, strong and regular.
Blood pressure cannot be obtained at this time.
Pt takes methadone. A cousin mentioned possible overdose based on large dose taken.
No chest pain or dizziness. He began breathing irregularly when the event occurred.
Do you experience any pain or discomfort right now?
Pt is not aware. Can you describe your name and the current location?
Pt responds with groans to pain, not with speech. GCS is 8.
EKG strips show sinus rhythm with no detectable ST changes.
We will administer naloxone via intranasal route to assist reversal of opioid effects.
This intervention is based on Overdose Management Protocol 7-1.
Pt is resisting placement of NPA. He seems agitated but not combative yet.
Now SpO2 is 97%. Improving oxygen saturation.
Blood pressure is 142/61. Getting more accurate readings now.
Respiratory rate has improved to 12 with assisted breathing.
Pulse has decreased to 71, still regular but lower.
Beginning monitoring with 4 lead EKG. No new abnormalities detected.
4 lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with consistent findings.
Skin was pale at initial assessment.
Eye pupils are 3 mm, equal and reactive. No unequal or fixed response.
Pt is not indicating any current pain or discomfort upon assessment.
Pt is more alert now, responds to questions all four (A&Ox4).
Pt has normal gross use of all four extremities.
Initiating 12 lead EKG-no acute myocardial infarction changes.
Repeat 12 lead EKG remains normal with sinus rhythm-no new issues.
SpO2 is 100% on room air now.
Respiratory rate is 14. Airway is now adequately supported.
Pulse has decreased further to 63 and is stable.
Blood pressure is 139/74. Pt is now stable now.
Administering second dose of 1 mg naloxone via nasal route.
Pt's respirations are now 16 with normal chest rise.
You are stable and better. Would you like transport to the hospital for further evaluation?
Pt refuses transport and chooses to return home against medical advice.
We explained all risks of refusal. Pt has comprehended and still refuses transport.
Pt signed the AMA form. You will be standing down, not being transported.
Full documentation and witness of AMA refusal completed.
If symptoms return or worsen, you must call an ambulance immediately.
Pt is safely secured on the stretcher and assisted into the ambulance.
Pt refused blood glucose check. No signs of hypoglycemia at present.
Skin is now dry, warm, and normal in color and texture.
Pt has chosen to go home against medical advice.
Overdose Management Protocol 7-1 is complete. You are cleared if needed later.
